records.ety: [AS. ranc strong, proud; cf. D. rank slender, Dan. rank upright, erect, Prov. G. rank slender, Icel. rakkr slender, bold. The meaning seems to have been influenced by L. rancidus, E. rancid.]

defs: Luxuriant in growth; of vigorous growth; exuberant; grown to immoderate height; as, rank grass; rank weeds.
quotes: And, behold, seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good.

defs: Raised to a high degree; violent; extreme; gross; utter; as, rank heresy.
Causing vigorous growth; producing luxuriantly; very rich and fertile; as, rank land.
Strong-scented; rancid; musty; as, oil of a rank smell; rank-smelling rue.

quotes: That rides so rank and bends his lance so fell.

records.ety: [OE. renk, reng, OF. renc, F. rang, fr. OHG. hring a circle, a circular row, G. ring. See Ring, and cf. Range, n. & v.]

defs: A row or line; a range; an order; a tier; as, a rank of osiers.
quotes: Many a mountain nigh Rising in lofty ranks, and loftier still.

defs: A line of soldiers ranged side by side; -- opposed to file. See 1st File, 1 (a).
quotes: Fierce, fiery warriors fought upon the clouds, In ranks and squadrons and right form of war.

defs: Grade of official standing, as in the army, navy, or nobility; as, the rank of general; the rank of admiral.
An aggregate of individuals classed together; a permanent social class; an order; a division; as, ranks and orders of men; the highest and the lowest ranks of men, or of other intelligent beings.
Degree of dignity, eminence, or excellence; position in civil or social life; station; degree; grade; as, a writer of the first rank; a lawyer of high rank.
quotes: These all are virtues of a meaner rank.

defs: Elevated grade or standing; high degree; high social position; distinction; eminence; as, a man of rank.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Ranked (rkt); p. pr. & vb. n. Ranking.]

defs: To place abreast, or in a line.
To range in a particular class, order, or division; to class; also, to dispose methodically; to place in suitable classes or order; to classify.
quotes: Ranking all things under general and special heads.
Poets were ranked in the class of philosophers.
Heresy is ranked with idolatry and witchcraft.

defs: To take rank of; to outrank.
To be ranged; to be set or disposed, as in a particular degree, class, order, or division.
quotes: Let that one article rank with the rest.

defs: To have a certain grade or degree of elevation in the orders of civil or military life; to have a certain degree of esteem or consideration; as, he ranks with the first class of poets; he ranks high in public estimation.
